prompt:
Take the provided reference image and reproduce it PIXEL-FAITHFULLY — same dual-state composition (LEFT: hand peeling release backing off the device's adhesive face with vial attached and hanging down + curved transition arrow + RIGHT: simplified man's shoulder/deltoid with the balloon-shaped device adhered to it with red button up and vial hanging down), same line-art style on pure white background, same line weight, same hand poses, same balloon-shaped device silhouette, same vial connection (vial flush-seated at the bottom of the device's lower protrusion), same arrow, same overall layout. DO NOT change the composition, the hand poses, the arrow, the line weight, the device shape, the vial, the right state, or the style.

THE ONLY MODIFICATION — LEFT STATE: hide the red button from view. In the reference's left state (peeling), the red button is partially visible on the device. But in this view we are looking at the BACK / ADHESIVE side of the device — the side that will stick to the skin. The red button is on the OPPOSITE face (the FRONT / TOP face) and should therefore be HIDDEN from view in this orientation. Redraw the left state so the device shows ONLY its adhesive back face: a plain white circular (with balloon-extension) silhouette with subtle texture indicating the sticky adhesive surface, the release-paper backing being peeled off as before, and NO red showing anywhere on the device in this state. The red button is fully on the opposite side of the device, occluded by the device's body.

RIGHT STATE: keep the red button VISIBLE as before (the device is placed on the deltoid with the red button facing UP toward the viewer, so red is correctly shown there). This state remains unchanged.

Do not modify anything else — no changes to the hands, the peeling action, the man's shoulder, the arrow, the device shape, the vial, the line weight, the background, or the style. Pure black line-art on white with the solid red raised button as the only color (visible only in the right state, hidden in the left state). No frame, no border around the canvas — image fills to the edges.
